title: Functions
description: Rust functions use ownership and borrowing for parameters — understanding when to pass by reference (&), mutable reference (&mut), or value is critical. Master fn declarations, return types, and closures for clean interview code.

sections.title: fn declaration and return types
sections.explanation: Declare functions with **fn name(params) -> ReturnType**. The last expression without a semicolon is the implicit return. You can also use explicit `return`. Parameter types must always be specified. Rust functions are snake_case by convention.

exercise.prompt: Write a function `multiply` that takes two i32 parameters and returns their product. Write a function `is_positive` that takes an i32 and returns bool. Call both and print the results.

validation.message: Define both functions with explicit parameter and return types

exercise.hint: is_positive should check n > 0. The last expression without semicolon is returned.

sections.title: Ownership and borrowing in parameters
sections.explanation: Pass by **reference** (`&Vec<i32>`) to read without taking ownership. Pass by **mutable reference** (`&mut Vec<i32>`) to modify in place. Pass by **value** to transfer ownership (rarely wanted for collections). In interviews, borrow (&) by default — it avoids unnecessary clones and ownership issues.

exercise.prompt: Write `find_max` that borrows a &Vec<i32> and returns the max value. Write `double_all` that takes &mut Vec<i32> and doubles every element in place. Print the max, call double_all, then print the vector.

validation.message: Use & for read-only borrow and &mut for mutable borrow, multiply by 2

sections.title: Closures
sections.explanation: Closures are anonymous functions defined with `|params| body`. They can capture variables from their enclosing scope. Use them with iterator methods like `.map()`, `.filter()`, and `.sort_by()`. Closures are essential for writing concise interview code in Rust.

exercise.prompt: Define a closure `square` that squares an i32. Use it with .map() to square all elements of [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Define a `threshold` variable of 10 and use a closure with .filter() to keep only squared values above it. Print the results.

validation.message: Define the square closure and use it with .map(), then filter with the threshold

exercise.hint: Filter with **x > threshold. The closure captures threshold from the enclosing scope.

sections.title: Returning multiple values with tuples
sections.explanation: Rust functions can return **tuples** to provide multiple values. Destructure with `let (a, b) = func()`. This is the idiomatic way to return multiple results — no need for out-parameters or wrapper structs for simple cases. Common in interview problems that return index pairs or (min, max).

exercise.prompt: Write a function `sum_and_count` that takes a &Vec<i32> and returns a tuple (i32, usize) of the sum and the count. Call it with [10, 20, 30] and print both values.

validation.message: Return a (i32, usize) tuple and destructure with let (s, c)

exercise.hint: The function body is already complete. Just run it — sum is 60, count is 3.
